original_abstract: Our understanding of secondary organic aerosol (SOA) formation within biomass burning plumes remains constrained by the incomplete identification and quantification of emitted non-methane organic compounds (NMOCs). To address this gap, we collected gaseous organic compounds on sorbent cartridges during laboratory burns as part of the fourth Fire Lab at Missoula Experiment (FLAME-4). These samples were analyzed using two-dimensional gas chromatography–time-of-flight mass spectrometry (GC × GC–ToFMS). Leveraging the high sensitivity and superior resolving power of this technique, we generated the most comprehensive biomass burning NMOC dataset to date, featuring measurements for 708 positively or tentatively identified species. We present calculated emission factors (EFs) for these compounds across six distinct fuel types, including conifer branches, grasses, agricultural residues, and peat. The number of compounds meeting our selection criteria ranged from 129 to 474 per burn and included numerous complex isomer groups. For instance, we identified 38 monoterpene isomers in emissions from coniferous fuels; the observed isomeric ratios align closely with those found in corresponding essential oils, suggesting that oil composition is a reliable predictor for fuel-dependent terpene emissions. Additionally, we detected and tentatively identified 11 sesquiterpenes, representing the first reported speciation of these compounds in gas-phase biomass burning emissions. Finally, we evaluate the calculated EFs for all measured compounds within the context of their potential contribution to SOA formation.
humanized_abstract: Secondary organic aerosol (SOA) processes in biomass burning plumes are not yet fully understood due to limited knowledge of primary emissions of non-methane organic compounds (NMOCs). In the 4th Fire Lab at Missoula Experiment (FLAME-4) study, we collected gaseous organic compounds on sorbent cartridges during laboratory burns of six very different fuels: coniferous and deciduous tree biomass, grassland fuels, agricultural residues, peat, and shrubland fuels. Using 2D GC×GC-ToFMS, we characterized in detail the complex mixtures of gas-phase biomass burning NMOCs. The resulting dataset has the highest number of identified species to date with 708 species identified/tentatively identified. Emission factors for all identified species are provided. With 129–474 identified species per burn, the emission factors were calculated for a large number of different species (including many individual isomers of the same parent formula) and several complex isomer groups of compounds (e.g. 38 monoterpene isomers were identified in coniferous fuel emissions and their isomeric ratios are similar to those reported for essential oils from the corresponding trees, thus indicating the reliability of estimating fuel-dependent terpene emissions based on oil composition). In addition, we identified 11 sesquiterpenes in the biomass burning emissions for the first time. The potential of identified species to form SOA is discussed based on their calculated emission factors.
